content:
In this paper: Coverings and ring-groupoids, it is said that the fundamental groupoid of a topological ring is a ring-groupoid i.e. a ring-object in the category of groupoids. But the fundamental groupoid of a topological ring is a rather trivial object since it is a thin category.

Indeed: Let RRR be a topological ring. First, we show that π1(R,0)≃{1}\pi^1(R,0) \simeq \{1\}π1(R,0)≃{1}.

Let γ:[0,1]→R\gamma:[0,1] \rightarrow Rγ:[0,1]→R be a path from 000 to r∈Rr \in Rr∈R. Define H:[0,1]×[0,1]→RH:[0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow RH:[0,1]×[0,1]→R by H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s)H(s,t)=i(t)\gamma(s)H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s). Then HHH is a continuous map. Moreover, H(s,0)=i(0)γ(s)=0γ(s)=0H(s,0)=i(0)\gamma(s)=0\gamma(s)=0H(s,0)=i(0)γ(s)=0γ(s)=0 and H(s,1)=i(1)γ(s)=1γ(s)=γ(s)H(s,1)=i(1)\gamma(s)=1\gamma(s)=\gamma(s)H(s,1)=i(1)γ(s)=1γ(s)=γ(s). We deduce that [γ]=[e0][\gamma]=[e_0][γ]=[e0​].

Now, let a,b∈Ra,b \in Ra,b∈R, we have an homeomorphism u:R→Ru:R \rightarrow Ru:R→R given by u(r)=r−au(r)=r-au(r)=r−a. It gives a group isomorphism ϕ:π1(R,a)≃π1(R,0)\phi:\pi_1(R,a) \simeq \pi_1(R,0)ϕ:π1​(R,a)≃π1​(R,0) defined by ϕ([γ])=[γ−a]\phi([\gamma])=[\gamma-a]ϕ([γ])=[γ−a] with inverse given by ϕ−1([τ])=[τ+a]\phi^{-1}([\tau])=[\tau+a]ϕ−1([τ])=[τ+a]. We deduce that π1(R,a)≃{1}\pi_1(R,a) \simeq \{1\}π1​(R,a)≃{1} for every a∈Ra \in Ra∈R.

Finally, let a,b∈Ra,b \in Ra,b∈R and let γ,γ′:[0,1]→R\gamma,\gamma':[0,1] \rightarrow Rγ,γ′:[0,1]→R be two paths from aaa to bbb. We obtain a path γ∗(γ′)−1\gamma * (\gamma')^{-1}γ∗(γ′)−1 from aaa to aaa. We have [γ∗(γ′)−1]=[γ][γ′]−1=1[\gamma * (\gamma')^{-1}]=[\gamma][\gamma']^{-1}=1[γ∗(γ′)−1]=[γ][γ′]−1=1. Thus [γ]=[γ′][\gamma]=[\gamma'][γ]=[γ′].

We conclude that the fundamental groupoid of RRR is isomorphic as a category to the poset whose objects are path-components of RRR and where a∼ba \sim ba∼b iff aaa and bbb are in the same path-component.

Should I conclude that this paper does not contain any interesting content? Maybe there is something interesting that I don't understand or maybe my proof above is wrong.
I'm even more intrigued when I see that this paper has been cited 33 times.

You're reminding me of this:

  • Tom Leinster, Holy crap, do you know what a compact ring is?

This result implies that a compact Hausdorff topological ring has a fundamental groupoid whose only morphisms are identity morphisms.
Thanks, this post looks interesting!

Jean-Baptiste Vienney said:

But the fundamental groupoid of a topological is a rather trivial object since it is a thin category.

You left out the word "ring".

Indeed: Let RRR be a topological ring. First, we show that π1(R,0)≃{1}\pi^1(R,0) \simeq \{1\}π1(R,0)≃{1}.

Let γ:[0,1]→R\gamma:[0,1] \rightarrow Rγ:[0,1]→R be a path from 000 to r∈Rr \in Rr∈R. Define H:[0,1]×[0,1]→RH:[0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow RH:[0,1]×[0,1]→R by H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s)H(s,t)=i(t)\gamma(s)H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s).

What's iii?

Argh, I first proved that the fundamental group of a path-connected topological ring is trivial where I used a path iii from 000 to 111 in RRR. And now I'm still using this path iii so that my proof is not correct!!
Well-spoted.
So I'm back to being confused about whether the fundamental groups and groupoids of topological rings are interesting or not.
I think I'm still right that the fundamental group of a path-connected topological ring is trivial.
However the fundamental group of a path-component of a topological ring should not necessarily be trivial.

Jean-Baptiste Vienney said:

So I'm back to being confused about whether the fundamental groups and groupoids of topological rings are interesting or not.

That's a fascinating question!

I can't think of any topological rings with fundamental groupoids that are not equivalence relations (=thin preorders). Tom showed they must be equivalence relations for topological rings that are compact Hausdorff. Have you proved it for topological rings that have a path from 0 to 1?
Yes, let me write the proof here again.
Wait, I must think about what is the proposition too.
Sure, don't rush.
Proposition: Let RRR be a topological ring such that 000 and 111 are in the same path component. Then π1(R,0)\pi_1(R,0)π1​(R,0) is trivial.

Proof: Let i:[0,1]→Ri:[0,1] \rightarrow Ri:[0,1]→R be a path from 000 to 111 in RRR. Let x∈Rx \in Rx∈R and let γ:[0,1]→R\gamma:[0,1] \rightarrow Rγ:[0,1]→R be a path from 000 to xxx. Define H:[0,1]×[0,1]→RH:[0,1] \times [0,1] \rightarrow RH:[0,1]×[0,1]→R by H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s)H(s,t)=i(t)\gamma(s)H(s,t)=i(t)γ(s). Then, HHH is a continuous map. Moreover, H(s,0)=i(0)γ(s)=0H(s,0)=i(0)\gamma(s)=0H(s,0)=i(0)γ(s)=0, H(s,1)=i(1)γ(s)=γ(s)H(s,1)=i(1)\gamma(s)=\gamma(s)H(s,1)=i(1)γ(s)=γ(s) so that [γ]=1[\gamma]=1[γ]=1 the identity of π1(R,0)\pi_1(R,0)π1​(R,0).
π1(R,0)\pi_1(R,0)π1​(R,0) is the fundamental group of RRR based at 0∈R0 \in R0∈R.
Great! So this implies that π1(R,r)\pi_1(R,r)π1​(R,r) is trivial for any basepoint r∈Rr \in Rr∈R.
Yes, I agree.
(You can use addition to translate a homotopy class of loops from rrr to 000.)
And that implies that the fundamental groupoid of a a topological ring with a path from 000 to 111 must be equivalent to a discrete category (one with only identity morphisms).
Yes, exactly.

I also think this result implies that any topological field has a fundamental groupoid that's equivalent to a discrete category, since in a topological field any continuous path from xxx to yyy with y≠xy \ne xy=x can be turned into a continuous path from 000 to 111.
So I am fairly optimistic that all topological rings have a fundamental groupoid that's equivalent to a discrete groupoid.... and if there's a counterexample I'd really like to see it!

John Baez said:

And that implies that the fundamental groupoid of a a topological ring with a path from 000 to 111 must be equivalent to a discrete category (one with only identity morphisms).

Did you use that every thin category is equivalent to a discrete category? Is it really true? Because what I proved at the beginning finally is that if a topological ring has a path from 000 to 111 then its fundamental groupoid is a thin category.
Oh but this fundamental groupoid is a thin groupoid not just a thin category.
And every thin groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category?

Did you use that every thin category is equivalent to a discrete category? Is it really true?

No, that's not true: any preorder is a thin category.

But the fundamental groupoid is a groupoid, and any thin groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category.

John Baez said:

I also think this result implies that any topological field has a fundamental groupoid that's equivalent to a discrete category, since in a topological field any continuous path from xxx to yyy with y≠xy \ne xy=x can be turned into a continuous path from 000 to 111.

Wait, I think that more precisely if a topological field has a nontrivial path component, then its fundamental groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category.

A thin groupoid is a fancy name for a category where there's at most one morphism from any object to any other object, and this morphism is invertible. So it's a category where any two objects either have no morphisms between them, or exactly one isomorphism. So it's equivalent to the discrete category on its set of isomorphism classes.

John Baez said:

A thin groupoid is a fancy name for a category where there's at most one morphism from any object to any other object, and this morphism is invertible. So it's a category where any two objects either have no morphisms between them, or exactly one isomorphism. So it's equivalent to the discrete category on its set of isomorphism classes.

Ok, thanks.

Jean-Baptiste Vienney said:

John Baez said:

I also think this result implies that any topological field has a fundamental groupoid that's equivalent to a discrete category, since in a topological field any continuous path from xxx to yyy with y≠xy \ne xy=x can be turned into a continuous path from 000 to 111.

Wait, I think that more precisely if a topological field has a nontrivial path component, then its fundamental groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category.

That's half of the argument.
What is the second half?

I'm saying that in a topological field there's either no continuous path from any element xxx to any y≠xy \ne xy=x (in which case the fundamental groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category) or there is such a path (in which case there's a path from 000 to 111, so your argument shows the fundamental groupoid is equivalent to a discrete category).
Ok, that's fine.
I feel it makes more natural the fact that a lot of topological fields are totally disconnected.
(At least, it seems related.)
So the fundamental groupoid of a topological field is always equivalent to a discrete category. I've just understood your reasoning :sweat_smile: .
If there is no path from any xxx to a y≠xy \neq xy=x then every path from xxx to xxx is equal to the constant path, else it would give a path from xxx to some y≠xy \neq xy=x.
It must tell us something about the fundamental groupoid of a topological integral domain.

Great! No worries.

By the way, it's possible that you could continue your argument to show that if a topological ring RRR has a (continuous) path from 000 to 111 then all its homotopy groups πn(R,r)\pi_n(R,r)πn​(R,r) are trivial. In this case we say RRR is "weakly homotopy equivalent" to a discrete topological space.

I think it would be fun to prove that every topological field is weakly homotopy equivalent to a discrete space.

And I almost feel like conjecturing that any topological ring is weakly homotopy equivalent to a discrete space! Certainly Tom showed any compact Hausdorff topological ring has this property - because they are totally disconnected. We have topological rings like R\mathbb{R}R and C\mathbb{C}C and R[x]\mathbb{R}[x]R[x] and R[x,y]/⟨x2−3y⟩\mathbb{R}[x,y]/\langle x^2 - 3y \rangleR[x,y]/⟨x2−3y⟩ that are not totally disconnected, but they're contractible, so they're weakly homotopy equivalent to a point.
I've never worked with higher homotopy groups ahah. You can think about it yourself if you want. Just say that I've contributed :sweat_smile:

I don't know how to handle a continuous maps Sn→RS^n \rightarrow RSn→R. Say I have a continuous path i:[0,1]→Ri:[0,1] \rightarrow Ri:[0,1]→R from 000 to 111. Could I obtain a continuous map γ:Sn→R\gamma:S^n \rightarrow Rγ:Sn→R from this?
Oh, maybe γ(x1,…,xn)=i(x1)\gamma(x_1,\dots,x_n)=i(x_1)γ(x1​,…,xn​)=i(x1​) for instance.

I can use the projections pi:Sn→[0,1]p_i:S^n \rightarrow [0,1]pi​:Sn→[0,1] for 1≤i≤n1 \le i \le n1≤i≤n to get continuous maps γi=i∘pi:Sn→[0,1]\gamma_i=i \circ p_i:S^n \rightarrow [0,1]γi​=i∘pi​:Sn→[0,1].
I didn't even know precisely the definition of πn(R,0)\pi_n(R,0)πn​(R,0). Let's write it here.
First we have to choose a base point aaa in SnS^nSn. Let's say a=(1,0,…,0)a=(1,0,\dots,0)a=(1,0,…,0).
Now πn(R,0)\pi_n(R,0)πn​(R,0) is the set of homotopy classes of continuous maps σ\sigmaσ from SnS^nSn to RRR such that σ(a)=0\sigma(a)=0σ(a)=0.
Let σ:Sn→R\sigma:S^n \rightarrow Rσ:Sn→R be a continuous map such that σ(a)=0\sigma(a)=0σ(a)=0.

We want to build a continuous map H:Sn×[0,1]→RH:S^n \times [0,1] \rightarrow RH:Sn×[0,1]→R such that H(x1,…,xn,0)=σ(x1,…,xn)H(x_1,\dots,x_n,0)=\sigma(x_1,\dots,x_n)H(x1​,…,xn​,0)=σ(x1​,…,xn​) and H(x1,…,xn,1)=0H(x_1,\dots,x_n,1)=0H(x1​,…,xn​,1)=0 and H(a,t)=0H(a,t)=0H(a,t)=0.
Random try: maybe take H(x1,…,xn,t)=i(t)σ(x1,…,xn)H(x_1,\dots,x_n,t)=i(t)\sigma(x_1,\dots,x_n)H(x1​,…,xn​,t)=i(t)σ(x1​,…,xn​).
Ok, it works.
Now I guess that all the πn(R,r)\pi_n(R,r)πn​(R,r) are isomorphic by translation.
<hr>

By the way, something else:

  • If RRR is a topological ring then every path γ\gammaγ from 000 to 111 in RRR gives a continuous map fff from R\mathbb{R}R to RRR (I think).
Define f(x)=⌊x⌋.1+γ(x−⌊x⌋)f(x)=\lfloor x \rfloor.1 + \gamma(x-\lfloor x \rfloor)f(x)=⌊x⌋.1+γ(x−⌊x⌋).
So that having a continuous path from 000 to 111 in RRR would be equivalent to having a continuous map from R\mathbb{R}R to RRR which preserves 000 and 111.

It would be nice if a topological ring RRR had a path from 000 to 111 iff it had a homomorphism of topological rings from R\mathbb{R}R to its center... (i.e. if it was a topological R\mathbb{R}R-algebra)

Note also, that:

  • if RRR is a topological ring with 0≠10 \neq 10=1, if there is a path γ\gammaγ from 000 to 111 in RRR then RRR is not totally disconnected since γ([0,1])\gamma([0,1])γ([0,1]) is a connected subspace of cardinal >1>1>1.

John Baez said:

And I almost feel like conjecturing that any topological ring is weakly homotopy equivalent to a discrete space! Certainly Tom showed any compact Hausdorff topological ring has this property - because they are totally disconnected. We have topological rings like R\mathbb{R}R and C\mathbb{C}C and R[x]\mathbb{R}[x]R[x] and R[x,y]/⟨x2−3y⟩\mathbb{R}[x,y]/\langle x^2 - 3y \rangleR[x,y]/⟨x2−3y⟩ that are not totally disconnected, but they're contractible, so they're weakly homotopy equivalent to a point.

I think that the fundamental groupoid of the topological ring of continuous maps from R\mathbb{R}R to R\mathbb{R}R (say with the product topology) is not equivalent to a discrete category! (or is it… ??)
Well, why do you think that?
Sorry, I’m wrong. The fundamental groupoid of every topological R\mathbb{R}R-algebra is equivalent to a discrete category.
Because if you have a loop γ\gammaγ from 000 to 000 then you have the following homotopy from the constant loop equal to 000 to γ\gammaγ: H(s,t)=tγ(s)H(s,t)=t\gamma(s)H(s,t)=tγ(s).
So the fundamental group based at 000 is trivial. By translation the fundamental group based at any point is trivial. It follows that the fundamental groupoid is thin.

To sum up we know that these three classes of topological rings have fundamental groupoid equivalent to a discrete category:

  • the topological R\mathbb{R}R-algebras
  • the topological fields
  • the compact topological rings
Maybe we can generalize the first two cases to the case of a topological algebra over a topological field
I think this is not so much being a field which matters but having a field sitting inside the ring.
Brr sorry, the case of a topological algebra over a topological field follows from the case when you have a path from 000 to 111 exactly in the same way as for a topological field. (I think)
Now, I’m no longer really sure about topological algebras over a topological field.
If the field is not R\mathbb{R}R, I don’t know what to do.

I think I see how to prove a dichotomy theorem: every topological field FFF is either contractible or there is no (continuous) path from xxx to yyy when x≠yx \ne yx=y.

Proof. If there's a path from xxx to yyy with x≠yx \ne yx=y, then we can subtract xxx from this path and get a path from 000 to y−xy - xy−x. Then we can divide that path by y−xy - xy−x and get a path γ\gammaγ from 000 to 111.

Then, given a path γ\gammaγ from 000 to 111, multiplication by 1−γ(t)1 - \gamma(t)1−γ(t) gives a homotopy from the identity map id:F→F\text{id}: F \to Fid:F→F to the map sending everything in FFF to zero. Thus RRR is contractible!

Now I'll speed up a bit and leave out details. It follows from the dichotomy theorem that there are two cases: either FFF is contractible or FFF is weakly homotopy equivalent to the discrete space with one component for each point in FFF. Since a contractible space is homotopy equivalent to a discrete space, in either case FFF is weakly homotopy equivalent to a discrete space!
This means that topological fields are 'boring' from a homotopy theorist's point of view - but there are two different ways for them to be boring.
